subject: math
source_title: New General Mathematics SSS 1 by M.F. Macrae et al
learning_objectives: Identify and describe the parts and regions of a circle
Calculate the length of an arc of a circle
Calculate the perimeter of a sector
Calculate the length of a chord and perimeter of a segment
Calculate the area of a sector
Calculate the area of a segment

sections.heading: Parts and Regions of a Circle
sections.content: A circle has a centre (the fixed middle point), a circumference (the outer curved boundary), a radius (any line from centre to circumference), a diameter (a chord through the centre), and an arc (a curved part of the circumference). Regions include: a sector (area between two radii and the arc), a segment (area between a chord and the arc), and a semi-circle (half the circle, bounded by a diameter).

sections.heading: Length of Arc and Perimeter of Sector
sections.content: For a circle of radius r with an arc subtending angle θ at the centre: arc length L = (θ/360°) × 2πr. The perimeter of sector = 2r + L = 2r + (θ/360°) × 2πr. Example: sector of radius 7cm, angle 108° → arc = (108/360) × 2 × (22/7) × 7 = 13.2cm; perimeter = 14 + 13.2 = 27.2cm.

sections.heading: Chord Length and Perimeter of Segment
sections.content: If a chord subtends angle 2θ at the centre of a circle of radius r, then chord AB = 2r sin θ. The perimeter of minor segment = chord + arc = 2r sin θ + (θ/360°) × 2πr, where θ here is the full sector angle. Example: in a circle of radius 6cm, a chord 3cm from the centre: cos θ = 3/6 = 0.5, so θ = 60° and the full angle = 120°; chord = 2 × 6 × sin 60° = 10.39cm; arc = (120/360) × 2π × 6 = 12.57cm.

sections.heading: Area of Sector
sections.content: Area of sector = (θ/360°) × πr². Example 1: sector of diameter 28cm (r=14cm) and angle 45° → area = (45/360) × (22/7) × 14² = 77cm². Example 2: circle PQR of area 72cm², angle POQ = 40° → area of sector POQ = (40/360) × 72 = 8cm².

sections.heading: Area of Segment
sections.content: Area of minor segment = area of sector − area of triangle = (θ/360°) × πr² − ½r² sin θ. Example: angle = 56°, r = 15cm → area = (56/360) × π × 225 − ½ × 225 × sin 56° = 110 − 93.26 = 16.74cm² ≈ 16.7cm².
